### Matplotlib (Python)
```python
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

plt.ylim(-12.5, 0)  # Set the y-axis range
plt.xlim(-12.5, 0)  # Set the x-axis range
plt.show()
```

### Seaborn (Python)
```python
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Example with a sample dataset
sns.set_style("whitegrid")
tips = sns.load_dataset("tips")

# Set the axis range
sns.set_context("notebook")
sns.set_style("darkgrid")
sns.set(rc={"figure.figsize":(10, 6)})
sns.set_style("white")
sns.set_style("darkgrid")

# Plot with specified axis range
sns.lineplot(data=tips, x="total_bill", y="tip", hue="sex")
plt.ylim(-12.5, 0)
plt.xlim(-12.5, 0)
plt.show()
```

### Plotly (Python)
```python
import plotly.express as px

df = px.data.tips()

fig = px.line(df, x="total_bill", y="tip", color="sex")
fig.update_layout(yaxis=dict(range=[-12.5, 0], xaxis=dict(range=[-12.5, 0]))
fig.show()
```

### ggplot2 (R)
```R
library(ggplot2)

# Example with a sample dataset
tips <- ggplot2::ggplot(tips, aes(x=total_bill, y=tip)) + geom_point(aes(color=sex))

# Set the axis range
ggplot2::ylimits(tips, lower=-12.5, upper=0)
ggplot2::xlim(tips, lower=-12.5, upper=0)
print(tips)
```

### Excel
1. Select the chart.
2. Go to the "Format" tab.
3. Click on "Chart settings."
4. Under the "Axes" section, set the minimum and maximum for the X and Y axes.
5. Set the minimum for the Y-axis to -12.5 and the maximum to 0.

